# Questions in Livestream 201

Live Stream #201: Back From The Land Of The Dead

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=565" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:25</a> | Why did you skip the so-called helicopter hieroglyphs on the Egypt trip? | He said they are not alien proof and belong to an overwritten inscription problem rather than mysterious ancient aircraft. | The tour skipped them because they are at Abydos, which was on the extended tour rather than the trip they took. He explained the famous shapes as a palimpsest: Seti I's inscription was overwritten by Ramesses II, and the overlapping signs accidentally resemble a helicopter or spacecraft. The Egyptian government even has an explanatory plaque, and the two royal claims are similar but not identical in emphasis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=936" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:36</a> | What kind of bridges did Egyptians have over the Nile and places like the Pi-Hahiroth area? | He said the exact bridge forms are uncertain because none survive, but texts and maps indicate that bridges did exist. | He said no ancient Egyptian bridges of this sort survive, so the exact construction cannot be known. Still, Egyptian bridges are illustrated, including on the Way of Horus map at Karnak, and he specifically connected the Pi-Hahiroth/Hebua crossing with a shallow area where a modest wooden footbridge of logs and planks would be plausible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=1106" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:26</a> | What methods improve prose and storytelling quality? | He recommended a threefold approach built around reading widely, practicing deliberately, and learning technique from strong writing. | His main advice was to read a lot and write a lot, because reading expands vocabulary, grammar, and turns of phrase, while practice improves writing over time. He cited the idea that writers may produce a large amount of weak work before writing well, and he also recommended a human critique partner who can identify blind spots that the writer misses. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=1424" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:44</a> | What arms and armor would the Israelites have used when coming out of Egypt? | He said essentially none at the moment of departure; they would have acquired weapons later. | He said the Israelites would not have left Egypt with formal armor and would have had to cobble weapons together afterward. Bows, arrows, slings, maces, and spears were plausible from Sinai supplies or bronze they carried, but armor was too expensive and uncommon even among Egyptian soldiers, being mainly for elites or royalty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=1663" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:43</a> | Where was the likely Yam Suph crossing, is it still there, and how deep was it? | He located it in the northwestern Sinai among the former Ballah Lakes, said those lakes were roughly 20 to 40 feet deep, and noted that the area has since dried up. | He placed the likely crossing in the northwestern Sinai, near the former Ballah Lakes between the Bitter Lakes and the estuary toward the Mediterranean. Those lakes were broad but not extremely deep, averaging about 20 to 40 feet. He added that they no longer exist because the Pelusiac branch silted up and the area north of the Bitter Lakes dried out. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=1918" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:58</a> | Does a bunny need another bunny to be happy? | He said it depends on the rabbit: some do well as bonded pairs, while others prefer being alone. | He answered that it depends on the individual rabbit. Some do better in bonded pairs, usually with an opposite-sex companion, though he immediately cautioned that rabbits should be spayed or neutered. Others are independent and do not play well with other rabbits, so a second rabbit is not automatically necessary. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=1959" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:39</a> | Do biblical covenant structures correlate more with Egyptian or Assyrian covenants? | He said it depends on which covenant and date are in view, with Pentateuchal material showing stronger Egyptian and Hittite parallels and later material more Assyrian relevance. | He said the answer depends on the specific biblical covenant and its date. Pentateuchal covenants correlate more closely with Egyptian and Hittite material, while Iron Age covenants are more relevant to Assyrian sources. For deeper study he recommended Kenneth Kitchen and Paul Lawrence's work on treaty, law, and covenant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=2063" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:23</a> | What is going on with the young man in Mark 14:51-52? | He said the man's identity is uncertain, but the scene most clearly fits the wider theme of disciples abandoning Jesus. | He corrected the reference to Mark 14:51-52 and said the identity of the young man is unknown. He listed possibilities, including a symbolic representation of the disciples' abandonment, Mark inserting himself into the story, or a known eyewitness, but stressed that commentaries differ and the safest point is that the scene reinforces Jesus being abandoned. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=2260" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:40</a> | Are there sacrifices in the Messianic Kingdom according to Ezekiel, and why? | He said sacrifices do appear in Ezekiel's vision, and they need not all be sin offerings because sacrifices can also express praise, thanksgiving, and ritual worship. | He said Ezekiel's vision does include sacrifices, but that does not mean they are all for sin. The sacrificial system includes thanksgiving, oath-related, ritual purification, and other offerings in addition to sin offerings. He framed the Messianic Kingdom sacrifices as praise, thanksgiving, and worship rather than a denial of Christ's work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=2404" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:04</a> | What is the difference between a sacrifice and an offering? | He said an offering is something owed, while a sacrifice is more than what is owed and usually involves the offered item being consumed. | He distinguished them from the giver's perspective: an offering is something owed, while a sacrifice goes beyond what is owed and is meant to cost the giver. He also emphasized that sacrifices are normally consumed or burned in a way ordinary offerings need not be. In his summary, all sacrifices are offerings, but not all offerings are sacrifices. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=2615" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:35</a> | If Adam had not eaten the fruit and only Eve had, would the Fall still have happened? | He said the corporate Fall would not have happened unless both federative heads of humanity fell together; otherwise it would be more like the fall of some angels. | He argued that the Fall, in the corporate sense, requires both Adam and Eve as humanity's representative heads to fall together. If only one had sinned, he thought it would resemble the angelic fall, where some fell and others did not. The whole species falls because both high-priestly representatives of humanity participate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=2873" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:53</a> | What should someone do when they keep falling into the same sin and fear they cannot enter heaven? | He urged prayer, avoiding tempting settings, practicing spiritual disciplines, relying on grace, and pursuing growth rather than expecting sinless perfection. | He said Christians still struggle with sin and that maturity often makes people more aware of their sin rather than less. His practical counsel was to leave environments where temptation repeatedly wins, pray, read Scripture, attend a local congregation, and practice spiritual disciplines. He emphasized God's grace, growth in sanctification, and obedience rather than despair or an expectation of sinless perfection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=3220" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:40</a> | What was the best thing you ate on the Egypt trip? | He said camel in onion gravy was the standout food, comparing it favorably to very good beef. | He said the standout was camel, prepared this time in a rich onion gravy. He described it as tender, meaty, and better than the camel he had tried before, comparing the flavor to excellent beef rather than chicken. He also mentioned trying more desserts and a green soup, but the camel was the favorite. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=3423" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:03</a> | Are meat camels retired work camels or raised just for meat? | He said they use young camels selected from the same stock that could otherwise become work animals, not old retired camels. | He said meat camels are young animals, not retired work camels, because retired camels are not valuable even for meat. The animals are not raised in a completely separate meat-only category; rather, some young camels from the same general stock are selected for meat while others become work camels. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=3634" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:34</a> | What was the weirdest thing you saw in Egypt? | He described seeing a small girl lift a broken-down tuk-tuk while its wheel was being repaired. | He described seeing a tuk-tuk broken down at the side of the road after a wheel came off. A small girl was holding or lifting the vehicle while the wheel was being repaired, which he presented as the strangest sight of the trip. He joked from that scene into chat banter, but the core observation was the improvised repair. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=3848" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:08</a> | What is going on in Mark 9:49-50? | He explained the saying in terms of preserving covenant seriousness and the testing, preserving role of salt imagery. | He treated the passage as a mixed metaphor drawn from salted and burned offerings. Salt represented purification, while fire represented testing, so he took the saying as both internal purification and external testing. The exhortation to have salt in oneself means maintaining personal purity and then living at peace with others. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=4033" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:13</a> | Can salt actually become unsalty? | He said Roman mined salt could lose its saltiness when the salt dissolved out of a rock-and-salt mixture, leaving only useless stones. | He explained that mined salt could be mixed with rocks and pebbles, unlike cleaner evaporated sea salt. People might put the mixture in a cloth bag and dip it into food, dissolving the salt out while leaving the rocks behind. Once the salt had leached out, the remaining material was effectively unsalty and useless. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=4259" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:59</a> | What can ancient Near Eastern diplomacy teach about avoiding conflict in the Middle East? | He said ancient diplomacy was mixed, but economic cooperation and mutually beneficial trade were usually better paths than war. | He cautioned that ancient diplomacy was not always successful; sometimes it produced treaties and trade agreements, and sometimes it failed or led to war. The more useful lesson he drew was that economic cooperation is generally preferable to war because it can enrich both sides. He also connected war to greed and taking what people want instead of asking. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=4830" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:30</a> | Is there evidence for advanced Late Hittite cavalry doctrine, and how did it compare with Egypt's military? | He said the Hittites had sophisticated military traditions, though chariots remained central, and that Egypt and the Hittites were peer powers. | He said the Hittites had access to horseback riding and related technology through Hurrian-linked vassals and therefore had a relatively sophisticated cavalry tradition. Still, chariots were better adapted for the main warfare context, with heavier Hittite chariots and lighter, faster Egyptian chariots reflecting different doctrines. He described the two militaries as peer powers whose long conflict produced no clear winner. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=5412" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:12</a> | Was Moses reluctant to appeal to Pharaoh because Egyptian courts beat diplomats? | He thought Moses was more likely worried about being recognized for a crime than about being beaten, but said the midwives were likely beaten before questioning. | He said Moses' reluctance was probably more about being recognized as someone accused of a crime than fear of a beating, since royal-family status likely protected him. By contrast, he thought the Hebrew midwives probably were beaten before questioning because Egyptian interrogation assumed beatings helped secure truthful answers. He also noted that Egyptian beatings applied to women and men alike. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=5702" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:02</a> | What did Egyptians pay pyramid builders with? | He said pyramid workers were paid in food rations such as beef, fish, wine, oil, and bread, with surplus tradeable for other needs. | He said workers were paid in rations of beef, fish, wine, oil, and bread, and that the Giza workers were well looked after compared with many other laborers. He mentioned Mark Lehner's excavation of the workers' village and evidence for beef, knuckle-bone soup, salted fish, wine, oil, bread, and medical supplies. Surplus rations could be traded for clothing, shoes, and other needs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=5942" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:02</a> | Do you agree that Acts was written before Paul's death? | He was cautious, saying he had not studied the issue deeply but thought a pre-death date was possible. | He said he had not looked deeply into the scholarship on Acts and therefore could not give a firm answer. He saw a pre-death date for Paul as possible and said he did not know of direct evidence requiring a later date, though it would be early. More broadly, he would place most New Testament writings in the first century. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=6004" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:04</a> | Why do Hebrew words for God and man sometimes take the definite article after Genesis 4? | He said the article can shift the sense between a name-like use and a qualitative or generic use, with context determining how much weight to place on it. | He worked through Genesis examples and said the presence or absence of the article can shift the usage from a name-like "Adam" toward "the man" or humanity, but the difference is not always large. He compared it to capitalization distinctions in English when "God" is used as a name or qualitatively. Context still determines whether the text means Adam, the man, or humanity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=6616" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:16</a> | If God cannot change his mind, is there a better word than "regret" in Genesis 6:6? | He said the Hebrew has a broad range and may convey being sorry or consoling oneself; he framed it as a change in God's position toward sin, not a change in God's nature. | He said the Hebrew terms have broad ranges, including ideas such as being sorry, grieving, or consoling oneself. He distinguished a change in God's essence or nature from a change in God's position toward sin, arguing that God remains immutable while responding to human evil. He suggested "console himself" or "was sorry" may capture the sense better than a simple modern idea of changing one's mind. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=8186" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:26</a> | Did ancient people see the physical realm and spiritual realms as separate or as an inseparable unity? | He said the evidence is limited, but ancient people often treated the realms as overlapping while still distinct. | He said the evidence is difficult because ancient texts do not give much abstract metaphysical philosophy. From what can be inferred, Egyptians and other ancient people saw physical and spiritual realms as distinct but overlapping, with ghosts of the dead and malevolent spirits operating near the living. He compared it to overlapping realms rather than complete separation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=8404" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:04</a> | What are your thoughts on James B. Pritchard's Ancient Near Eastern texts anthology? | He said it is a classic and sometimes useful anthology, but many translations are dated and should be checked against newer work when possible. | He called Pritchard's anthology a classic collection that was good in its day and can still be useful, especially for searching translated ancient texts. However, he said many translations are dated, some show theological coloring, and the volume is not his first stop when newer translations exist. He advised using it with caution rather than taking it at face value. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=8584" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:04</a> | What do you think about someone limiting service to God's kingdom to only their own household? | He said that is too narrow because the Great Commission and spiritual gifts point beyond one's household, though caregiving can create real exceptions. | He said limiting kingdom service only to one's household contradicts the Great Commission's command to make disciples of all nations. That does not mean everyone must be a minister, missionary, or street evangelist, but service should extend beyond the home. He used a stay-at-home parent as an example: care for a household matters, but church service, ordinary public interactions, and other relationships still reach beyond the family. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=9112" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:52</a> | In Genesis 1:26, is God speaking to the Divine Council or to another person of the Godhead? | He preferred a trinitarian reading, allowed the cohortative "royal we" as possible, and rejected the Divine Council reading because verse 27 says God creates humanity in God's image. | He said the main options are Divine Council, trinitarian, and cohortative or royal-we readings. He favored the trinitarian reading and considered the cohortative reading acceptable, but rejected the Divine Council interpretation because verse 27 identifies God as the one creating humanity in God's image. Humans are not made in the Divine Council's image, so that reading does not fit the immediate context for him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=9569" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:29</a> | What do you make of the supposed underground structures beneath the pyramids that were making headlines? | He rejected the claim as implausible, especially the idea of two-kilometer-deep engineered structures under the pyramids. | He dismissed the claim as absurd, focusing especially on the proposed spiral structures descending about two kilometers beneath the pyramids. He compared that depth to an oil well and questioned the practical feasibility of carving or engineering such a pit under a pyramid. He also noted that the images or scans being circulated were suspect and did not make the theory credible to him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=10029" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:09</a> | If hazy weather blotted the sun and Venus appeared as a tiny dot, what might Egyptians have said? | He said they might describe Ra or the sun as darkened, but the conditions would need to be unusually hazy and dark. | He said the likely Egyptian description would be that Ra, or the sun, was darkened or blotted. He cautioned that the conditions would need to be more than ordinary haze; it would have to be very hazy and dark for the phenomenon to be visible or noteworthy in the way proposed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=10210" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:10</a> | When will you start learning Latin? | He said he wishes he had time and was annoyed that a promised Latin class had not materialized. | He said he wished he had time to learn Latin and that both he and his producer were annoyed because Trinity had listed Latin in the catalog but never actually offered the class. He added that Latin is interesting and useful, but he did not give a definite plan to begin studying it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=10342" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:22</a> | Which alien tinfoil-hat theory is strongest? | He said most such theories are poorly constructed, but he is open to alien life existing somewhere even while doubting alien visitation claims. | He said most alien conspiracy theories are badly constructed, especially the ones associated with ancient-alien claims, so he did not identify a strong example. He distinguished that from the broader possibility of alien life, which he considered plausible because of the scale of the universe and because he sees no biblical reason other life could not exist. He doubted contact or visitation claims because of the enormous distances involved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=10637" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:57:17</a> | What was the recent Egypt tour like? | He summarized visits to Giza, museums, Saqqara, Elephantine, Karnak, Luxor, the Ramesseum, Rekhmire's tomb, and Medinet Habu, emphasizing sites relevant to Egypt and the Bible. | He described the trip as fun and focused on sites relevant to Egypt and the Bible. In the early part of the account he mentioned the pyramids, the Egyptian Museum, the Merneptah or Israel stela, Amarna displays, the ritual sites around Menkaure's funerary temple, the Bent Pyramid, Memphis, and Saqqara. He also framed the group as small and close-knit and said he tried to make sure they saw the important biblical-history locations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=11975" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:35</a> | Do you know of good commentary sets that are not Reformed? | He said many commentaries come from Reformed circles, recommended choosing commentaries case by case, and mentioned Hermeneia, NICOT, Word, and Jewish Publication Society resources with caveats. | He said many commentary writers come from Reformed circles, and he did not recommend relying on one set for everything. Hermeneia is not Reformed but is more liberal and not necessarily Christian in its commitments. He suggested evaluating commentaries case by case, praised NICOT and parts of Word, and said Jewish Publication Society resources can be useful for the Old Testament, though all sets have limits. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=12290" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:50</a> | If I am making an Egyptian temple in Minecraft, which mobs must I keep out to avoid defiling it? | He joked in game terms but answered from temple access rules: commoners and outsiders stay out, craftsmen may enter outer areas, and inner spaces are for priests. | He answered through temple access rules translated into game language. Commoners, farmers, slaves, and outsiders should be kept out, while craftsmen could be allowed into the peristyle or outer areas. Anyone past that point, especially into the inner court or hypostyle-like spaces, should be priestly personnel; he also joked that undead or clearly defiling mobs should stay out. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=12444" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:27:24</a> | What are your thoughts on the Acts of Thecla? | He said he was not familiar with it in detail, but after checking it live he treated it as late second-century apocrypha. | He began by saying he had not really heard of it or was not familiar enough to comment from memory. After looking it up during the stream, he identified it as apocryphal, with earliest attestation around AD 190, and therefore placed it in the late second century rather than treating it as early or canonical material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=12550" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:10</a> | Have you seen Gavin Ortlund's video on the canon of Scripture, and what is your opinion? | He had not seen that specific video, but said canon history is complicated and that he usually finds Ortlund strong on early Christian history and canonicity. | He said he had not seen that particular video, so he could not evaluate its exact argument. More generally, he said the history of the canon is complicated and was often recognized more than formally declared except in moments of controversy. He usually agrees with Ortlund on early Christian history and canonicity because that is Ortlund's academic field, even though he does not agree with him on every theological-development issue. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=12703" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:43</a> | What exactly is happening in Matthew 27:51-53, and is it symbolic? | He argued it is not merely symbolic; the torn veil shows sacred space opening, and the raised saints display the power of Christ's resurrection. | He rejected a merely symbolic reading. The torn temple veil represents sacred space being opened and the Holy Spirit or divine presence moving into the world as Christ's kingdom is inaugurated. He also argued that Matthew's wording about the bodies of many saints being raised is evidence of Christ's power over death, a representative resurrection witness rather than every tomb opening. He warned that making this only symbolic weakens the textual force of "appeared to many." |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=13850" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:50</a> | How would you respond to people who say life is not worth living because of suffering? | He said suffering calls for spiritual, medical, or mental-health help rather than self-destruction, and criticized pressure toward euthanasia. | He said people who feel this way need help rather than self-destruction: spiritual counseling, medical attention, or mental-health care depending on the kind of suffering. He also criticized euthanasia rhetoric, saying that pressure often comes from others judging a suffering person's life as not worth living. He warned against language that makes people feel like burdens and said ending one's life is not the best option. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JZy3BGzlUxo?t=14806" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:06:46</a> | Does Osiris beat his farm workers? | He said Egyptian afterlife texts suggest people want to work in Osiris's fields because the alternatives are worse; he does not appear to beat them, but they are still expected to work. | He said that, from what can be gathered, people want to work in Osiris's fields because the alternatives are much worse, such as fiery or punishing afterlife locations. On that basis, Osiris does not appear to beat his farm workers, though workers are still expected to work in the fields. |
